explanation: This is a single-panel scatterplot with approximately 16 labeled data points encoded using four distinct colors to represent different categories. The direct labeling of each point adds moderate visual density, and the multiple color categories require cognitive effort to distinguish, but the overall layout remains a standard 2D scatterplot with clean gridlines and no additional panels or complex encodings.

This is a horizontal grouped bar chart with 19 countries and two data series (Deceased and Living), using two distinct colors and stacked/grouped bars. The moderate number of categories and dual-encoding increases complexity beyond a simple bar chart, but it remains a single-panel, straightforward visualization with clear labels and a standard layout.

This is a clean infographic featuring a simplified world map with five color-coded continental regions and corresponding numerical labels. The design is sparse with minimal visual elements—just colored silhouettes, five data annotations, and a title—resulting in low perceptual demand. The use of color encoding adds slight complexity beyond a plain chart, but the overall layout remains simple and uncluttered.

This is a grouped bar chart with two categories (refinance and home purchase) across 12 time periods, using two distinct colors. The chart includes annotation callouts, a descriptive subtitle, dotted gridlines, and source attribution, adding modest complexity beyond a simple bar chart but remaining a straightforward single-panel visualization without dense layering or multiple encoding dimensions.

This stacked horizontal bar chart displays 19 categories with 3 color-encoded segments each, creating a moderately dense visualization. The large number of labeled rows, multi-segment bars, legend, and detailed axis labeling add perceptual demand. While the chart type is standard, the quantity of data series and text labels pushes complexity above a typical simple bar chart.

This stacked area chart features 8 distinct country categories rendered in similar warm/brown hues, requiring perceptual effort to distinguish individual bands. The legend with 8 entries, labeled axes, dense year markings, and the overlapping color gradients add moderate complexity. The similar color palette makes differentiation challenging, pushing complexity slightly higher than a standard area chart.

This visualization shows a scatter plot with three color-coded clusters (red, green, blue) using oriented elliptical glyphs that encode additional dimensions through size and rotation. The multi-color encoding and varying glyph orientations add moderate complexity, but the absence of axes, labels, legends, and grid lines keeps the overall cognitive demand at a moderate level.

This parallel sets diagram shows three hierarchical levels (Class, Sex, Survived) with crossing flow bands connecting categories. The overlapping teal and green bands create visual intricacy and perceptual demand as viewers must trace connections across levels. The moderate number of categories (2 per level) and dual color encoding keep complexity at a mid-range level.

This dual-line chart displays two time series with dense, noisy data spanning multiple years, enhanced by several annotation callout boxes highlighting specific values and a highlighted spread region between the lines. The combination of jagged lines, multiple labeled data points, explanatory text blocks, and the shaded area adds moderate visual complexity beyond a simple line chart. The annotations and color-coded elements require careful perceptual parsing but the overall layout remains a single-panel design.

This biplot-style scatterplot uses multiple visual encodings simultaneously: dot position in 2D space, dot size, color gradient (dark to orange representing calorie values), directional vector arrows labeled with variable names, numerical axis annotations, and specific data point labels. The color legend bar adds an additional layer of complexity. While moderately information-dense, it remains a single-panel view without extreme overplotting.

The image consists of concentric circular rings in a highly regular, repetitive pattern using only two color hues (dark green and reddish-brown). While there are approximately 15–20 distinguishable rings creating some visual density, the pattern is entirely symmetric and contains no labels, axes, legends, or data encodings beyond the radial gradient structure, keeping cognitive demand low.

This contour/density plot features multiple simultaneous encodings: a continuous color scale with gradient fill, numerous overlaid contour lines of varying density, a distinct blue overlay curve, and dual axes on both x and y dimensions. The combination of color mapping, dense contour lines (particularly clustered in certain regions), multiple axis scales, and a detailed colorbar legend creates moderate-to-high visual complexity in a single panel.

The visualization contains two side-by-side 3D heart mesh models with color-mapped surfaces (orange-to-red gradient encoding), a grid overlay, and the right panel adds dense green diamond glyphs at each mesh node. The combination of 3D rendering, color encoding, mesh structure, and glyph overlay creates moderate-to-high visual complexity, compounded by the dual-panel layout and orientation cube indicators.

This visualization consists of a dense 6×10 grid of small heatmap/matrix panels, totaling 60 individual sub-visualizations, each containing internal grid structure with varying purple-green color patterns. The sheer number of panels, the internal complexity within each cell, and the multi-encoding (color intensity, spatial position, grid patterns) create very high perceptual and cognitive demand. The repetitive yet varied patterns across rows and columns require significant visual processing to compare and interpret.

This visualization combines three overlapping encodings: a continuous color heatmap, contour isolines, and a dense vector/arrow field spanning the entire plot area. The hundreds of directional arrows, irregular contour curves, and spectral color gradient all compete for attention simultaneously, creating substantial perceptual demand. Despite being a single panel, the multi-layer encoding and high density of graphical elements push complexity well above a standard heatmap or contour plot.

This is a dense network graph with hundreds of nodes and edges distributed across the entire canvas, creating significant visual complexity. Multiple overlapping edge connections, varying cluster densities, and four annotated subgraphs (SG1–SG4) highlighted with red circles add additional layers of perceptual demand. The combination of dense interconnections in the center, scattered peripheral clusters, and annotation overlays places this firmly in the high-complexity range.

This image depicts an extremely dense 3D urban landscape visualization composed of hundreds of overlapping rectangular block forms rendered in varying grayscale shades, filling nearly the entire frame. The curved fisheye-like perspective adds additional spatial complexity, and the sheer density of elements with varying heights, sizes, and orientations demands significant perceptual effort to parse. There are no axes or labels to simplify interpretation, leaving the viewer to navigate the intricate visual mass alone.

This is an extremely dense scientific flow visualization rendered in 3D perspective, combining multiple simultaneous visual encodings: color mapping across several variables (blue, red, yellow, green), complex swirling LIC-style flow textures, and a dense field of directional glyphs scattered across the right portion. The intricate vortex structure layered with volumetric color fields and thousands of individual glyph elements creates very high perceptual and cognitive demand throughout the entire image.

This visualization is an extraordinarily dense space-filling display (likely a treemap or pixel-based hierarchy) containing thousands of tiny visual elements arranged in a multi-scale hierarchical grid. The density escalates toward the bottom where elements become nearly indistinguishable, creating extreme perceptual demand. Multiple color encodings (green, cyan, gray) are embedded throughout this massive field of micro-elements, making it one of the most cognitively demanding visualization types possible.

This 3D scientific visualization features dense overlapping streamlines forming complex vortical patterns, multiple distinctly colored 3D tensor glyphs (ellipsoids with dots), red and blue directional markers scattered throughout, and a reflective floor plane—all within a transparent bounding box. The combination of 3D spatial depth, multiple simultaneous encoding channels (shape, color, size, orientation, streamline curvature), and four distinct vortex regions creates high perceptual and cognitive demand. While it is a single view rather than a multi-panel dashboard, the intricate layering of visual elements pushes the complexity score well above average.

This is a simple grouped bar chart with only three bars, each featuring hatched fill patterns and small red error bars. The visualization contains minimal visual elements: a title, two axis labels, gridlines, and three labeled categories, resulting in very low perceptual and cognitive demand.

This is a single bar chart with approximately 20 bars showing both positive and negative values. The complexity is moderately elevated due to the large number of country labels (rotated at an angle), the mix of positive and negative bars, and the need to read diagonal text. However, it uses only a single color and encoding channel, keeping overall complexity moderate.

This is a simple dual-line chart with only two data series plotted over 11 years (2000–2010), featuring clear axis labels, a small legend, and two value annotations. The layout is clean with minimal visual encoding complexity, a straightforward title and caption, and no overlapping or dense elements.

This is a single-panel bar chart with approximately 20 bars decreasing in height from left to right, all using a single black color encoding. While it has more bars than a minimal chart, there is only one visual encoding (bar height) and no additional complexity from color, multiple series, or annotations. The faded axis labels add slight perceptual difficulty but don't significantly increase complexity.

This stacked bar chart contains approximately 30 bars, each divided into 3 color-coded segments (blue, dark red, yellow), creating considerable visual density. The addition of a legend, gridlines, and numerous axis tick marks on both axes adds to the perceptual load. While it remains a single-panel visualization, the high number of bars and multi-segment stacking pushes it into moderate-to-high complexity territory.

This image shows five curved paths with red endpoint dots, each displaying the same 'IEEE Information Visualization 2005' text at progressively smaller scales. The visual complexity is relatively low—it's a technical demonstration of text-on-curve rendering rather than a data-rich visualization. The repeated motif and limited color palette (green text, red dots, black curves) keep cognitive demand modest.

This is a donut chart with 5 labeled segments showing electrical power generation shares, accompanied by a title, descriptive paragraph, and source citation. The multiple color-coded segments with percentage labels add moderate complexity, but it remains a single chart type with straightforward encoding. The text-heavy header adds some visual weight but doesn't significantly increase perceptual demand.

This is a matrix visualization with 5 columns and 16 rows, where each cell contains segmented bars encoded with three shades of blue to indicate research focus levels (high, medium, low). The combination of many categorical labels, multiple columns, and the within-cell color encoding creates moderate-to-high perceptual demand. The legend adds an additional decoding layer, pushing complexity above a standard grouped bar chart.

This is a stacked area chart with three data series spanning roughly 30 years, featuring a legend, gridlines, axis labels, and a title with source attribution. The layered encoding adds moderate perceptual demand compared to a simple single-series chart, but it remains a single-panel visualization without complex multi-encoding or dense elements, placing it in the mid-range of complexity.

The image presents two side-by-side map visualizations of the Netherlands — a choropleth map with 20+ labeled numeric values across many colored regions, and a tile/cartogram version using hundreds of small colored squares. The dual-panel layout, rich multi-color encoding, numerous text annotations, and irregular geographic shapes combined with the grid-based abstraction create a moderately high perceptual demand.

The visualization features numerous elliptical glyphs encoding multiple variables (orientation, size, color intensity) scattered across the canvas, with two zoom inset boxes connected by dashed lines adding navigational complexity. A color scale legend, dashed outer border, and the varied glyph orientations create moderate perceptual demand. The multi-encoding approach and inset detail views push this above a standard scatterplot in complexity.

The image contains a dense grid of approximately 60-70 repeated directional glyphs on a black background, each encoding orientation and magnitude through color (orange/red to green/blue) and shape. The high element density and subtle variations in glyph orientation across the field create significant perceptual demand. While the repeating structure provides some regularity, the multi-encoding (color, angle, shape) and sheer number of elements push complexity well above average.

The visualization contains three panel waffle/unit charts with dense grids of small colored squares across multiple rows, requiring careful visual parsing. Multiple shades of blue encode different values across rows labeled 2–6, and the repeated panel structure for three time points adds moderate complexity. While the layout is systematic, the high cell density and multi-panel arrangement elevate it above a simple chart.

This visualization combines multiple complex visual encodings: a streamgraph temporal flow chart at the top with colored bands representing different data streams, two large circular word clouds with embedded pie charts, and coordinated annotations connecting the flow to the detail views. The density of text in the word clouds, the multi-color encoding scheme, and the hybrid chart types create high perceptual and cognitive demands across the entire image.

This visualization is a dense hierarchical tree/network diagram featuring numerous nodes (circles), rectangular label elements, and orthogonal connecting edges spread across the full canvas. Multiple overlapping rectangular bounding boxes and deeply branching sub-trees create significant structural complexity. The irregular layout with clusters of varying density adds considerable perceptual demand.

This timeline visualization uses multiple encoding channels simultaneously — color (7 categories), marker shape (circles vs. triangles), and position — across 11 horizontal user tracks. The dense clustering of differently colored and shaped data points along each user's timeline requires significant perceptual effort to parse, and the 7-item legend adds further cognitive load. The combination of multi-category, multi-shape, and multi-row layout pushes complexity well above a standard line chart.

This geographic map visualization layers multiple encodings simultaneously: a topographic shading background, colored circles with dual encoding (hue for elevation trend, size for standard error), and numerous overlaid line types (major rivers, river basins, ICESat glacier boundaries, sub-region boundaries). Additional complexity comes from numerous text annotations with statistical values, a multi-component legend, geographic grid lines, and small directional indicators on data points, all demanding significant perceptual effort to parse.

This is an extremely dense genomic oncoprint visualization featuring dozens of rows across multiple categorical sections (clinical variables, subtypes, copy number alterations, somatic mutations, pathway annotations) and over 100 patient columns. Multiple simultaneous color encodings (red, blue, black, orange, green, purple, pink) represent different data types, and the layout includes complex labeling on both axes plus a multi-element legend at the bottom, creating very high perceptual and cognitive demand.

This visualization combines a geographic map with dozens of densely overlapping concentric circle glyphs encoded in multiple colors, representing different sound types across Jerusalem. The extensive legend with numerous categories, the complex layered transparency of overlapping sound zones, street networks, and landmark labels all contribute to very high perceptual and cognitive demand. The multi-encoding approach (color, size, position, pattern) creates intricate visual complexity throughout the composition.

This phylogenetic timeline visualization combines multiple complex elements: a branching cladogram in blue, layered geological time periods across three rows at the bottom (with over 20 named stages), multiple shaded regions (Romer's gap, Hexapoda gap), an embedded fossil photograph, dashed reference lines, numbered markers, and taxonomic labels. The dense bottom axis with numerous geological period names and the interplay between temporal scale and phylogenetic tree structure create significant cognitive demand.

This visualization displays an extremely dense triangular mesh with thousands of individual elements, each color-encoded on a blue gradient scale to represent scalar values. The sheer density of the mesh creates high perceptual complexity, with intricate color variations and structural geometry (T-shaped cutout) embedded within. The combination of the dense tessellation pattern, continuous color mapping, and embedded structural features demands significant cognitive effort to parse.

This visualization contains an extremely dense matrix of data with hundreds of country rows and dozens of Olympic year columns, all encoded with multiple colors (magenta, cyan, gray) representing different metrics. The sheer volume of data points, tiny text labels for each country and year, and multi-color encoding creates very high perceptual and cognitive demand. The overall information density approaches the maximum complexity range.

This visualization presents a highly complex multi-scale network diagram featuring several densely interconnected cluster nodes with internal mesh structures, color-coded node chains connecting clusters, zoom-in callout panels, and highlighted nodes with dashed circles. The combination of multiple visual encodings (color, size, position, connectivity), nested detail views, and the dense edge networks within clusters creates substantial perceptual and cognitive demand.

This is an extremely dense multi-panel visualization system featuring at least five coordinated views (Base Text, Overview, Page View, Line View, Word View) with numerous labeled components (A–G). Each panel contains dense grids of color-coded cells, text annotations, and multiple visual encodings including color, size, and spatial position. The sheer quantity of information elements, panel interactions, and layered visual channels pushes this to near-maximum complexity.

This visualization combines multiple complex view types: small matrix/pixel representations on the left, a dense flow/parallel-coordinates diagram with many crossing colored ribbons in the center, and four word cloud panels on the right. Multiple encoding channels (color, font size, line thickness, position) are employed simultaneously across coordinated views, creating very high perceptual and cognitive demand. The density of text labels, overlapping curves, and multi-panel layout pushes complexity near the top of the scale.

This is a dense network/graph visualization featuring hundreds of nodes and an extremely large number of crossing edges forming multiple overlapping clusters. The combination of hierarchical tree structures, dense interconnected hubs, and long-range arcing connections creates high perceptual demand. The monochromatic scheme reduces some complexity, but the sheer density of nodes and edges with multiple structural patterns pushes the score high.
